extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The provided exemplars do not explicitly detail multiplicities or hierarchical structures of Digital Twin instances. However, the **GreenhouseDT** exemplar employs a **self-adaptive digital twin** where both the physical greenhouse and its digital counterpart exhibit behavioral adaptation, suggesting a **one-to-one twinning relationship** with potential for dynamic synchronization. The **Air Quality Management** exemplar uses a **Raspberry-Pi-based sensor network** for real-time data collection, implying a **decentralized sensor-to-twin integration** with a centralized digital twin model hosted via **Microsoft Azure tools**. The **Lab Scale Gantry Crane** system (mentioned in the context diagram) lacks explicit details on twin multiplicities but follows a **C4 Model architecture**, which could theoretically support **modular twin instances** for different crane components (e.g., puck transport, positioning). No explicit coordination mechanisms or decentralized twin hierarchies are described in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The **GreenhouseDT** exemplar employs an **ad-hoc orchestration** with **InfluxDB** for time-series data storage, **ActiveMQ** for message brokering, and **virtual machines** for deployment, utilizing **prerecorded data** to simulate physical behavior. The **Air Quality Management** exemplar integrates **AutomationML-based digital twin models** with **Microsoft Azure** for pipeline implementation, featuring **runtime visualization, simulation, and prediction** services. The **Lab Scale Gantry Crane** system (implied in the context diagram) lacks explicit constellation details but aligns with the **ad-hoc approach** described in the discussion, where **containerization/virtualization** (e.g., Docker/Kubernetes) is implied for modularity. No commercial digital twin framework is used; instead, **custom integration patterns** dominate, with **single-board computers (Raspberry-Pi)** acting as edge nodes for physical-digital synchronization.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: The **GreenhouseDT** exemplar integrates with **InfluxDB** (time-series database) and **ActiveMQ** (message broker) for bidirectional data exchange, relying on **virtual machine prerecorded data** for offline synchronization. The **Air Quality Management** exemplar leverages **AutomationML** for model exchange, **Microsoft Azure** for cloud-based processing, and **Raspberry-Pi sensors** for real-time physical data ingestion, with no explicit mention of enterprise system dependencies. The **Lab Scale Gantry Crane** system (contextualized in the discussion) is implied to use **C4 Model architecture** for software-hardware integration but lacks details on interoperability standards (e.g., OPC UA, MQTT) or enterprise system dependencies. No explicit horizontal integration patterns (e.g., federated twins, microservices) are documented.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin system for the **GreenhouseDT** exemplar employs **behavioral adaptation** of both the physical greenhouse and its digital twin, utilizing **InfluxDB** for time-series data storage and **ActiveMQ** for message brokering. The digital twin integrates **prerecorded data** within virtual machine images, enabling real-time behavioral modeling. For the **Air Quality Management** exemplar, the digital twin is built using **AutomationML** for model representation, with **Microsoft Azure tools** facilitating simulation, prediction, and runtime visualization. The **Lab Scale Gantry Crane** exemplar does not explicitly mention specific model types (e.g., geometric, behavioral, or analytical) or data management approaches in the provided documents. The **Air Quality Management** exemplar uses a **Raspberry Pi** with sensors for real-time data collection, while **GreenhouseDT** relies on virtualized environments for data processing. Model relationships are implied through **ad-hoc integration** of data sources (e.g., sensors, prerecorded datasets) with digital twin pipelines, but explicit model relationships (e.g., coupling methods) are not detailed in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The **Air Quality Management** exemplar features a **Raspberry Pi** equipped with a **set of sensors** for monitoring indoor air quality, likely including environmental parameters such as CO₂, temperature, humidity, and particulate matter (PM2.5/PM10) for virus spread prevention. The **GreenhouseDT** exemplar does not explicitly describe its sensing infrastructure, but it implies the use of sensors within the greenhouse for behavioral adaptation. The **Lab Scale Gantry Crane** exemplar does not mention specific sensing components in the provided documents. The **Air Quality Management** exemplar’s sensors are integrated with the digital twin pipeline via **Azure tools**, enabling real-time data transmission. Sampling frequencies, spatial distribution, or accuracy specifications are not detailed in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: The **GreenhouseDT** exemplar emphasizes **behavioral adaptation** of both the physical and digital systems, suggesting iterative validation through real-time feedback loops. However, explicit **fidelity requirements**, **verification methods**, or **uncertainty quantification** (e.g., error detection/correction mechanisms) are not described. The **Air Quality Management** exemplar focuses on **runtime visualization, simulation, and prediction**, implying model validation via **Azure-based pipelines**, but no technical details (e.g., validation metrics, uncertainty sources) are provided. The **Lab Scale Gantry Crane** exemplar does not address fidelity or validity considerations in the provided documents. Quality assurance processes are not mentioned for any exemplar.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction:
The **Physical-to-Virtual Interaction** in the lab-scale gantry crane Digital Twin system involves continuous data acquisition from the **Physical Crane** hardware to the **Digital Twin** components via the **Physical Crane Interface** (a Python program). Key details include:

- **Data Types Transmitted**:
  - Crane state metrics (e.g., position, velocity, acceleration of the gantry and puck).
  - Controller feedback (e.g., motor currents, encoder readings).
  - Execution trajectory data (e.g., planned vs. actual path coordinates).

- **Communication Protocols**:
  - Direct low-level communication with crane controllers (exact protocol unspecified but implied to be hardware-specific, e.g., serial/USB or proprietary bus interfaces).

- **Transmission Frequencies**:
  - Continuous logging via the **Logging Application**, though exact frequency not specified. The **Continuous Validation Service** implies real-time or near-real-time data ingestion for trajectory comparison.

- **Event Triggers**:
  - Triggered by crane movements (e.g., puck displacement, controller updates) or scheduled polling intervals (unspecified).

- **Data Quality Assurance**:
  - No explicit mention of preprocessing (e.g., filtering, normalization) or validation rules, though the **Continuous Validation Service** suggests post-acquisition validation against simulated trajectories.

- **Data Integration Mechanisms**:
  - Data is ingested into a **TimescaleDB** database via the **Logging Application**, which acts as the central repository for all system-generated data. The **MQTT Broker** facilitates asynchronous signaling between services (e.g., Logging Application → Database).

  **Note**: Preprocessing steps (e.g., sensor calibration, noise reduction) are not documented.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction:
The **Virtual-to-Physical Interaction** in this system is mediated by the **Control Application**, which generates and executes commands for the **Physical Crane** via the **Physical Crane Interface**. Key aspects include:

- **Command Types**:
  - Movement trajectories (optimized via the **Trajectory Generator Service**) with kinematic constraints (e.g., zero-residual swinging at endpoints).
  - Control parameters (e.g., speed, acceleration profiles) derived from the simulation model.

- **Control Message Formats**:
  - Commands are likely serialized as structured data (e.g., JSON or protocol buffers) to ensure compatibility with the **Physical Crane Interface** (Python-based).

- **Validation and Authentication Mechanisms**:
  - No explicit authentication is mentioned, but the **Continuous Validation Service** validates executed trajectories against simulated predictions using distance metrics (e.g., Euclidean distance between planned vs. actual paths). Threshold breaches trigger alerts via the **Dashboard**.

- **Feedback Systems**:
  - Closed-loop feedback is implied through the **Continuous Validation Service**, which compares real-time measurements with simulated trajectories. Operators are notified via the **Dashboard** if deviations exceed thresholds.

- **Conflict Resolution**:
  - Not explicitly documented, but the **Control Application** likely prioritizes real-time trajectory execution over research simulations (e.g., via the **Simulation Service**), as the physical system operates independently of the Digital Twin when needed.

- **Command Execution Confirmation**:
  - No explicit confirmation mechanism is described, though the **Logging Application** records executed trajectories for post-hoc validation.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection:
The **Digital Twin Technical Connection** infrastructure relies on the following components and protocols:

- **Communication Protocols**:
  - **MQTT Broker**: Used for inter-service signaling (e.g., Logging Application → Database, Trajectory Generator → Control Application). MQTT ensures lightweight, publish-subscribe communication with QoS levels unspecified.
  - **Database Integration**: TimescaleDB (a PostgreSQL extension) stores time-series data, with no explicit protocol specified for direct database access (likely REST/HTTP or native PostgreSQL APIs).
  - **Physical Crane Interface**: Custom Python-based interface for low-level hardware communication (protocol unspecified but likely hardware-specific).

- **Network Topology and Architecture**:
  - No explicit topology described, but the **MQTT Broker** suggests a centralized messaging hub connecting all Digital Twin services (Dashboard, Trajectory Generator, Simulation Service, etc.). The **Physical Crane Interface** bridges the physical system to the Digital Twin via a dedicated connection.

- **Bandwidth and Latency Requirements**:
  - Not specified, but real-time trajectory validation (via **Continuous Validation Service**) implies low-latency requirements for MQTT and database operations. TimescaleDB’s time-series optimization suggests efficient querying for real-time data.

- **Reliability and Redundancy Mechanisms**:
  - No redundancy mechanisms are documented. The system relies on the **Continuous Validation Service** for fault detection (e.g., trajectory deviations) but lacks explicit failover or backup protocols.

- **Security Protocols**:
  - Not mentioned. Assumed to be minimal (e.g., default MQTT security or local network isolation), as no encryption, authentication, or access control measures are described.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment:
The **Digital Twin Hosting and Deployment** infrastructure is not explicitly detailed, but inferred from the described components:

- **Hosting Platform**:
  - **On-premise**: The system is tailored for a lab-scale gantry crane, implying local deployment (no cloud/edge deployment mentioned). The **MQTT Broker**, **TimescaleDB**, and Python services likely run on a single machine or a small cluster.

- **Computational and Storage Requirements**:
  - **Computational**: The **Trajectory Generator Service** and **Simulation Service** require computational resources for kinematic modeling and trajectory optimization (unspecified hardware specs). The **Continuous Validation Service** performs real-time distance metric calculations, implying moderate CPU requirements.
  - **Storage**: TimescaleDB stores time-series data for logging and validation. No capacity or retention policies are specified, but the system is designed for research purposes (likely small-scale data volumes).

- **Scalability Mechanisms**:
  - Not documented. The system is monolithic in scope (limited to a single gantry crane), with no mention of horizontal scaling (e.g., distributed MQTT brokers or sharded databases).

- **Deployment Models Supported**:
  - **Single-node deployment**: All services (Dashboard, Trajectory Generator, Simulation Service, etc.) are likely deployed as interconnected processes on a single machine. No containerization (e.g., Docker) or orchestration (e.g., Kubernetes) is mentioned.

- **Availability/Reliability Assurance**:
  - No redundancy or high-availability mechanisms are described. The system’s reliability depends on the **Continuous Validation Service** for anomaly detection but lacks proactive failover or backup strategies.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages:
The provided documents do not explicitly detail lifecycle phases for any of the Digital Twin systems (GreenhouseDT, Air Quality Management, or Lab Scale Gantry Crane). However, the **GreenhouseDT** exemplar implies a lifecycle involving:
  - **Development Stage**: Behavioral adaptation of both the physical greenhouse and its digital twin, utilizing pre-recorded data in virtual machine images for initial setup.
  - **Runtime Stage**: Continuous adaptation of the digital twin's behavior in response to real-time sensor data (InfluxDB) and message brokering (ActiveMQ).
  - **Transition Management**: Ad-hoc, as the exemplar lacks formalized governance frameworks but relies on containerization/virtualization for reproducibility.
  The **Air Quality Management** exemplar suggests lifecycle phases including:
  - **Design Stage**: Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) principles for digital twin construction, with AutomationML as the representation standard.
  - **Deployment Stage**: Raspberry Pi-based sensor integration for physical system monitoring.
  - **Runtime Stage**: Simulation, prediction, and visualization via Microsoft Azure tools (e.g., runtime analytics pipelines).
  The **Lab Scale Gantry Crane** system (mentioned in context) implies lifecycle phases via the C4 Model, though specifics (e.g., transition governance) are not detailed. No lifecycle governance frameworks (e.g., ISO/IEC 42010, IEEE 1471) are referenced in the documents.

The **GreenhouseDT** exemplar employs an **ad-hoc engineering process** with:
  - **Methodologies**: Behavioral adaptation of both physical and digital systems, leveraging pre-recorded data in virtual machine images for initialization.
  - **Quality Assurance**: Implicit via containerization (virtual machines) and message brokering (ActiveMQ) for data synchronization; no explicit testing frameworks mentioned.
  - **Version Control**: Hosted on GitHub, but no versioning strategies (e.g., Git tags, CI/CD pipelines) are specified.
  - **Evolution Management**: Ad-hoc updates via GitHub repositories; no formalized evolution strategies (e.g., model versioning, delta updates) are documented.

  The **Air Quality Management** exemplar follows:
  - **Methodologies**: Model-Driven Digital Twin Engineering (MDE) with AutomationML as the core representation standard.
  - **Quality Assurance**: Validation via Azure-based runtime pipelines (simulation/prediction) and Raspberry Pi sensor calibration.
  - **Version Control**: GitHub-hosted with no explicit versioning details.
  - **Evolution Management**: Ad-hoc updates via GitHub; no formalized strategies for model evolution (e.g., semantic versioning for AutomationML).

  The **Lab Scale Gantry Crane** system (contextual mention) lacks explicit details on twinning processes. No commercial frameworks (e.g., Siemens MindSphere, PTC ThingWorx) are used; instead, ad-hoc approaches dominate. **Not in Document** for the crane’s specific twinning process.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making:
The **Air Quality Management** exemplar provides **decision support capabilities** through:
  - **Insights Generated**: Real-time air quality metrics (e.g., CO₂, particulate matter) via Raspberry Pi sensors, processed via AutomationML models.
  - **Analytics/Processing**: Simulation and prediction pipelines implemented in **Microsoft Azure**, enabling scenario analysis (e.g., virus spread modeling).
  - **Decision Making**: Visualization tools for runtime monitoring; no explicit confidence measures (e.g., uncertainty quantification) are mentioned.
  - **User Interaction**: Web-based dashboards (Azure-hosted) for end-users to interpret insights.

  The **GreenhouseDT** exemplar lacks explicit decision support details beyond behavioral adaptation; no analytics or user interaction methods are described. **Not in Document** for insights/decision-making.

  The **Lab Scale Gantry Crane** system (contextual mention) implies insights via C4 Model architecture visualization but lacks specifics on analytics or decision-making processes. **Not in Document** for crane-related insights.

However, it relies on **InfluxDB** (a time-series database) and **ActiveMQ** (a message broker) for data exchange, which may introduce potential vulnerabilities if not properly secured (e.g., unencrypted data transmission, default credentials, or lack of role-based access control). The **Lab Scale Gantry Crane** system is described using the **C4 Model**, but no technical details on **security and safety** are provided in the context diagram or accompanying text. No mention of **cyber-physical security** (e.g., secure communication protocols for crane control), **authentication systems** (e.g., OAuth, API keys), or **fail-safe mechanisms** (e.g., emergency stop overrides, redundant controllers) is included. The system’s **cyber-physical integration** (e.g., puck movement automation) introduces potential risks such as **unauthorized control commands** or **data integrity violations**, but these are not addressed in the documentation.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
# Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar



Joost Mertens

_Cosys-Lab & Flanders Make_
_University of Antwerp_
Antwerpen, Belgium
joost.mertens@uantwerpen.be


_**Abstract**_ **—The research topic of digital twins has attracted a**
**large amount of interest over the past decade. However, publicly**
**available exemplars remain scarce. In the interest of open and**
**reproducible science, in this exemplar paper we present a lab-**
**scale gantry crane and its digital twin. The exemplar comprises**
**both the physical and digital side of the twin system. The physical**
**side consists of the physical crane and its controller. The digital**
**side covers the CAD models and kinematic model of the crane,**
**and provides services for optimal control, historical data logging,**
**data visualization and continuous validation. We used this setup**
**as use case in several previous publications where its functionality**
**was validated. It is publicly available and only relies on other**
**freely available and commonly used software, this way we hope**
**it can be used for future research or education on the topic of**
**digital twins.**
_**Index Terms**_ **—exemplar, digital twin, open-source, cyber-**
**physical-system**


I. I NTRODUCTION

The digital twin paradigm connects an actual system with a
digital counterpart consisting of models of parts of the actual
systems and services derived therefrom. During operation
data flows from the actual to the digital system to feed the
twin’s services. In turn, the twin’s services improve the actual
system’s workings. While the paradigm is applicable for both
physical and digital actual objects [1], in practice we see it
mainly applied to physical ones [2], [3].
Because of this bi-directional interaction, demonstrating
research results requires relevant use cases. Such research
results could be new synchronization methods, better or faster
data streaming, improved services and the like. The exemplar
in this paper for example, was used as a demonstrator for the
following digital twin related research: continuous validation
methods [4], [5], fault detection methods [6] and DarTwin, a
notation for system evolution [7].
In our experience, the use cases themselves typically do
not contain new research, yet they demand a substantial development effort on the researcher’s part. Finding of-the-shelf
exemplars in literature is rare, as the use cases in papers are
often not described in a reproducible manner, are costly to set
up, or are not openly available because they contain intellectual
property. Having readily available off-the-shelf exemplars in
various domains would help reduce development overhead,
and additionally also also help support the generalizability of
research results.

Given the industry relevance of digital twins, education
is another field for which well described exemplars can be



Joachim Denil
block_2_docs_retrieved:
_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.


_D. Discussion_


The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins
in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They
commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy
dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses


2 [https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X](https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X)
3 [https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT](https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT)
4 [https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality DigitalTwin Exemplar](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar)























Fig. 2. Context diagram of the system.


a commercial digital twin framework, while the others are
more ad-hoc.

In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less
ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.
The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more
logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our
opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.


III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_

small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_


Figure 2 shows the context diagram of the system. In
essence, it shows the high-level abstraction of what a digital
twin is, and two actors that interact with the system. The
operator is the actor that would operate and monitor this
system day to day, whilst the researcher can perform digital
twin research on it.
Figure 3 shows the container [6] diagram of the system. We
have limited ourselves to the most necessary relationships to
keep the diagram readable.
The gantry crane system, which can operate without digital
twin if needed, consists of five containers.
**Physical Crane:** the actual hardware of the crane.


5 [https://c4model.com/](https://c4model.com/)
6 Not a Docker container, a container is an abstraction in the C4 model for
something that must be there for the system to work.


Fig. 3. Container diagram of the system.



**Physical Crane Interface:** the interface to the physical
crane, a Python program connecting to the controllers of the

crane.

**Control Application:** a Python application that uses the
crane interface to control the crane.

**Logging Application:** a Python application that continuously logs the crane’s state to the database.
**Human Machine Interface:** a Python front-end for the
operator to configure and control the crane.
The digital twin consists of six containers. We’ll describe
them bottom up.

**Database:** a TimescaleDB database that contains all the data

created by the system and its various services.
**Dashboard:** a Grafana dashboard that visualizes data from

the database.

**Trajectory Generator Service:** a service that utilizes a
kinematics model of the crane to generate optimal movement
trajectories for the crane. Such optimal trajectories have no
residual swinging motion at the end.
**Simulation Service:** a service that implements a simulation
of the crane and it’s controllers. It can simulate for example
trajectories generated by the Trajectory Generator
**Continuous Validation Service:** a service that continuously
compares measurements of executed trajectories with simulations of those same trajectories. It calculates distance metrics
between them and compares the value with an acceptable
threshold. In case the threshold is breached operators are
notified via the dashboard and can take appropriate action.
**MQTT Broker:** an MQTT broker for signaling between
services.

Finally, we will not discuss the components of each of the
containers, but limit ourselves to the **Physical Crane** to give
block_5_docs_retrieved:
_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.


_D. Discussion_


The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins
in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They
commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy
dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses


2 [https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X](https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X)
3 [https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT](https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT)
4 [https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality DigitalTwin Exemplar](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar)























Fig. 2. Context diagram of the system.


a commercial digital twin framework, while the others are
more ad-hoc.

In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less
ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.
The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more
logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our
opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.


III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_
